lesson.title: Data Visualization
lesson.body: Matplotlib is the foundation: fig, ax = plt.subplots() creates a figure with axes. Seaborn builds on matplotlib with statistical plots: sns.histplot(), sns.scatterplot(), sns.boxplot(), sns.heatmap(). Pandas DataFrames have a built-in .plot() method. Key customization: ax.set_title(), ax.set_xlabel(), ax.set_ylabel(), plt.legend(), plt.tight_layout().

rooms.question: Complete the code to create a figure with axes:
rooms.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, ax = plt.___(figsize=(10, 6))
ax.plot([1,2,3],[4,5,6])
plt.show()
rooms.explanation: plt.subplots() is the standard way to create a Figure and one or more Axes. Returns (fig, ax) or (fig, axes_array) for multiple subplots.

rooms.question: Complete the code to create a seaborn scatter plot:
rooms.code:
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'x':[1,2,3],'y':[4,5,6],'group':['A','B','A']})
sns.___(data=df, x='x', y='y', hue='group')
rooms.explanation: sns.scatterplot(data=df, x=..., y=..., hue=...) creates a scatter plot with color-coded groups. The hue parameter automatically adds a legend.

rooms.question: When should you use sns.heatmap()?
answers: To visualize a correlation matrix or 2D grid of values
rooms.explanation: Heatmaps visualize matrix-like data with color. Most commonly used with df.corr() to show pairwise correlations between numerical features.

rooms.question: Order the steps to create a publication-quality plot:
rooms.explanation: Create axes → draw data → label → save. tight_layout() prevents label clipping. dpi=300 gives high-resolution output.

rooms.question: Complete the code to plot a correlation heatmap:
rooms.code:
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({'a':[1,2,3],'b':[3,2,1],'c':[1,3,2]})
sns.___( df.corr(), annot=True, fmt='.2f')
rooms.explanation: sns.heatmap(df.corr()) visualizes the correlation matrix. annot=True shows numeric values; fmt='.2f' formats to 2 decimal places.

boss.name: The Unlabeled Axis
boss.taunt: Your plot has no title, no axis labels, and no legend. No one knows what they're looking at.

questions.question: Complete the code to create 2 side-by-side subplots:
questions.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, ___, figsize=(12, 5))
questions.explanation: plt.subplots(1, 2) creates 1 row, 2 columns of axes. Returns (fig, axes) where axes is an array of 2 Axes objects.

questions.question: What is the key difference between plt.show() and plt.savefig()?
answers: show() displays in notebook/window; savefig() writes to disk — call savefig() BEFORE show() or plt.close()
savefig() shows in window; show() saves
questions.explanation: show() renders and clears the figure. If you call show() before savefig(), you save an empty figure. Always save before showing.

questions.question: Complete the code to add a legend to a plot:
questions.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot([1,2,3],[4,5,6], label='Line A')
ax.plot([1,2,3],[6,5,4], label='Line B')
ax.___()
questions.explanation: ax.legend() displays the legend using the label= parameter set on each plot. You can also pass loc='upper right' etc. to control placement.
